signedReleases.note: npm package published with provenance attestations.

secretsHandling.note: Connection string carries credentials. Pass via env / args; the server doesn't persist or log it.

credentialStorage.note: Connection string lives in process env / args. No on-disk credential cache.

oauthHandling.note: Plain Postgres auth via connection string.

dataResidency.note: Wherever your Postgres lives. Query results enter the LLM context — pay attention to data-classification before pointing this at production.

defaultPermissions.note: Whatever the connecting role can do. With a properly-scoped read-only role, this is small. With a default `postgres` superuser, this is everything.

destructiveTools.note: `query` is one tool; whether it's destructive depends on the SQL the agent generates and the role's permissions. Forks that add `execute` for DDL/DML increase blast radius materially.

promptInjectionExposure.note: Row contents the agent reads back can contain hostile instructions. Common in any user-generated text column (comments, descriptions). Treat all query output as untrusted text.

supplyChainSurface.note: Direct deps are MCP SDK and pg (the canonical Node Postgres client). Both have strong release hygiene.

rateLimitRisk.note: No MCP-layer rate limit. A long-running agent can run expensive queries; mitigations are at the Postgres role level (statement_timeout) not the MCP server.

verdictScope: Read-only agentic SQL over a Postgres database.
verdictNote: Use a dedicated Postgres role with SELECT-only and a short `statement_timeout`. Never connect with the `postgres` superuser or your app's role. For DDL/DML workloads, prefer migrations and code review — not an LLM with database write access.
